title: Theatre Square
description: Theatre Square in the capital city has a rectangular shape with size n x m meters. The mayor wants to cover the square with square granite flagstones of size a x a meters. Each flagstone must cover a full a x a area (no cutting). What is the minimum number of flagstones needed?
input_spec: The input contains three positive integers n, m and a (1 <= n, m, a <= 10^9).
output_spec: Print the minimum number of flagstones needed.

routes.name: Ceiling division
routes.description: Use ceiling division to compute tiles needed in each dimension, then multiply.

observations.title: Independent dimensions
observations.description: The number of tiles along the width and height can be computed independently, then multiplied.

hints.nudge: Think about tiling one row first. Then think about how many rows you need.
hints.question: If the square is n meters tall and each tile is a meters, how many tiles do you need vertically?
hints.formal: Tiles needed = ceil(n/a) * ceil(m/a). The dimensions are independent.

observations.title: Ceiling division without floats
observations.description: ceil(n/a) can be computed as (n + a - 1) / a using integer arithmetic, avoiding floating point errors.

hints.nudge: How do you round up in integer division?
hints.question: Regular integer division truncates. How can you adjust n before dividing to get ceiling instead of floor?
hints.formal: ceil(n/a) = (n + a - 1) / a in integer arithmetic. This avoids floating point precision issues.

observations.title: Use 64-bit integers
observations.description: n and a can be up to 10^9. The product ceil(n/a) * ceil(m/a) can exceed 2^31, requiring 64-bit integers.

hints.nudge: Check the constraints carefully. What's the maximum possible answer?
hints.question: If n = m = 10^9 and a = 1, how large is the answer? Does it fit in a 32-bit integer?
hints.formal: Max answer is 10^9 * 10^9 = 10^18, which exceeds 2^31. Use 64-bit integers (long long in C++, or Python handles it automatically).
